content.problemStatement:
A long school holiday has come, and you decided to visit the famous Byte Island. You know there are only two types of Bytelandians: Byteknights and Byteknaves. A Byteknight always tells the truth, whereas a Byteknave always lies.

It is known that there are N Bytelandians in the island, and now you meet all of them. You are curious about their types. Because you are a smart logician, you don't want to ask them questions that immediately reveal their types (that's too boring). Instead, to each Bytelandian you ask, "How many Byteknights are there here?"

To your surprise, they also don't answer your questions directly. Instead, the i-th Bytelandian answers of the form "The number of Byteknights here is between ai and bi, inclusive". You record all answers in your pocket note.

Now that you have collected all information you need, determine the type of each Bytelandian.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with a line containing an integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 50000) – the number of Bytelandians. Then N lines follow, each containing two integers ai and bi (0 ≤ ai ≤ bi ≤ N) describing the range claimed by the i‑th person.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output two lines. The first line contains the number of different possible assignments modulo 1000000007. The second line contains the lexicographically smallest assignment as a string of N characters, where '1' denotes a Byteknight and '0' denotes a Byteknave. If there is no possible assignment, output an empty line as the second line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, there is only one Bytelandian who claims there is one Byteknight. Since this Bytelandian must be a Byteknight, the answer is 1. For the second test case, there are four Bytelandians, and the only possible solution is that all of them are Byteknaves. For the third test case, there are three Bytelandians, and the lexicographically smallest solution is that the first and third Bytelandians are Byteknights.

solution.approach: For a fixed total number of knights K (0 ≤ K ≤ N) the type of each person is forced: if K lies inside the claimed interval [ai, bi] the person must be a knight, otherwise he must be a knave. Therefore for a given K the assignment is uniquely determined and is consistent iff the number of forced knights equals K itself. Let cnt[K] be the number of intervals that contain K. The condition becomes cnt[K] = K. We compute cnt[K] for all K using a difference array (prefix sums) in O(N) time. All K satisfying cnt[K] = K are valid solutions. The number of solutions is the size of this set (modulo MOD). For the lexicographically smallest assignment we generate the binary string for each valid K (type_i = 1 iff K ∈ [ai, bi]) and keep the smallest string; the number of valid K is at most N+1, so the total work is O(N·|valid|) which is easily fast enough for the given limits.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) to build the prefix sums + O(N·S) to compare strings, where S is the number of valid K (S ≤ N+1). In practice S is very small, so the overall complexity is O(N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the difference array and the input intervals.
